---
title: "Diabetes Risk Analysis - Complete Reproducible Code"
author: "Vobo Godlove Watoh"
date: "`r Sys.Date()`"
output:
  html_document:
    toc: true
    toc_float: true
    theme: flatly
    highlight: tango
    code_folding: show
---

```{r setup, include=FALSE}
knitr::opts_chunk$set(echo = TRUE)
```


```{r}

# Load tidyverse for data manipulation (dplyr, tidyr) and visualization (ggplot2)
# This is the core package suite for most data analysis tasks in R
library(tidyverse)

# Load skimr for comprehensive data exploration summaries
# Provides a quick overview of variable types, missing values, and distributions
library(skimr)

# Load janitor for data cleaning utilities
# Functions include: clean_names(), remove_empty(), tabyl() for frequency tables
library(janitor)

# Load readr for efficient CSV file import
# read_csv() is faster than base R's read.csv() and provides better type inference
library(readr)

```


```{r}
# ================================================================
# PHASE I: DATA CLEANING
# ================================================================

# Load the Pima Indians Diabetes Dataset from local file
# Update the file path to match your directory structure if needed
Diabetes <- read_csv("~/Desktop/Data Analysis Project /Diabetes_Risk_Analysis/Data/diabetes.csv")

# View the dataset in a spreadsheet-like viewer (RStudio only)
view(Diabetes)

# Attach the dataset to allow direct column name access
# (Alternative: use data$column notation to avoid potential conflicts)
attach(Diabetes)

# Basic Dataset summary: min, max, mean, median, quartiles for each variable
summary(Diabetes)

# Comprehensive data exploration with skimr
# Shows variable types, missing values, means, standard deviations, histograms
skim(Diabetes)

```


```{r}
# ================================================================
# PHASE I(a): DETECT MEDICALLY IMPLAUSIBLE ZERO VALUES
# ================================================================

# Count how many zeros exist in each column
# This helps identify variables where zero is biologically impossible
colSums(Diabetes == 0)

# Specifically check physiological variables where zero is clinically implausible
# Glucose: plasma glucose cannot be 0 in a living individual
# BloodPressure: diastolic blood pressure cannot be 0
# SkinThickness: triceps skinfold thickness cannot be 0
# Insulin: serum insulin cannot be 0
# BMI: Body Mass Index cannot be 0
sapply(Diabetes[, c("Glucose", "BloodPressure", "SkinThickness", "Insulin", "BMI")],
       function(x) sum(x == 0))

# Converting impossible zeros to missing values (NA)
# This ensures we don't treat biologically impossible values as valid measurements
Diabetes_clean <- Diabetes

# Define variables to clean
vars <- c("Glucose", "BloodPressure", "SkinThickness", "Insulin", "BMI")

# Replace all zeros with NA in the specified columns
# lapply applies the replacement function to each column in vars
Diabetes_clean[vars] <- lapply(Diabetes_clean[vars], function(x) replace(x, x == 0, NA))

# Verifying that number of NA matches number of zeros
# This confirms our replacement worked correctly
colSums(is.na(Diabetes_clean))

# Calculate missing value percentage for each variable
# This helps decide which imputation strategy is appropriate
missing_pct <- round(colMeans(is.na(Diabetes_clean)) * 100, 2)
missing_pct
```


```{r}
# ================================================================
# PHASE I(b): MISSING DATA IMPUTATION (MEDIAN IMPUTATION)
# ================================================================

# Imputing Missing Values using median imputation
# Median is preferred over mean because:
# - It is robust to extreme values/outliers
# - Less sensitive to skewed distributions
# - Common in clinical research for its simplicity and transparency
Diabetes_inputed <- Diabetes_clean

# Loop through each variable and replace NA with the column median
for (v in vars) {
  Diabetes_inputed[[v]][is.na(Diabetes_inputed[[v]])] <- median(Diabetes_inputed[[v]], na.rm = TRUE)
}

# Verify no missing values remain after imputation
colSums(is.na(Diabetes_inputed))

# Saving the cleaned dataset for future use
# row.names = FALSE prevents saving row numbers as a separate column
write.csv(Diabetes_inputed, 
          "~/Desktop/Data Analysis Project /Diabetes_Risk_Analysis/Data/Diabetes_clean.csv", 
          row.names = FALSE)
```


```{r}
# ================================================================
# PHASE II(a): EXPLORATORY DATA ANALYSIS (EDA) WITH CLEAN DATA
# ================================================================

# Reload necessary packages for EDA (already loaded but explicit for clarity)
library(tidyverse)
library(ggplot2)
library(GGally)  # For ggpairs() - extended pair plots with correlation and distributions

# Load the cleaned dataset
Diabetes_inputed <- read_csv("~/Desktop/Data Analysis Project /Diabetes_Risk_Analysis/Data/Diabetes_clean.csv")

# Frequency table: 0 = No Diabetes, 1 = Diabetes
table(Diabetes_inputed$Outcome)

# Proportion of diabetic and non-diabetic participants
# This checks class balance for classification modeling
prop.table(table(Diabetes_inputed$Outcome))
```


```{r}
# ================================================================
# PHASE II(b): VISUALIZATION - DISTRIBUTION OF OUTCOME
# ================================================================

# Set working directory for saving output files
# Update this path to your desired output location
setwd("~/Desktop/Data Analysis Project /Diabetes_Risk_Analysis")

# Plot 1: Bar chart of Diabetes Outcome Distribution
# Shows the count of non-diabetic (0) vs diabetic (1) participants
plot1 <- ggplot(Diabetes_inputed, aes(x = factor(Outcome), fill = factor(Outcome))) +
  scale_fill_manual(
    name = "Diabetes Status",
    values = c("0" = "blue4", "1" = "red4"),
    labels = c("0" = "Non-Diabetic", "1" = "Diabetic")
  ) +
  geom_bar() +
  theme_minimal() +
  labs(
    title = "Diabetes Outcome distribution",
    x = "Outcome",
    y = "Count"
  )
plot1

# Save plot as high-resolution TIFF for publication
ggsave("Distribution_outcome.tiff", plot1)

```


```{r}
# ================================================================
# PHASE II(c): VISUALIZATION - AGE DISTRIBUTION
# ================================================================

# Plot 2: Histogram of Age Distribution by Diabetes Status
# Overlaid histograms with dodge position to compare age distributions
plot2 <- ggplot(Diabetes_inputed, aes(x = Age, fill = factor(Outcome))) +
  scale_fill_manual(
    name = "Diabetes Status",
    values = c("0" = "blue4", "1" = "red4"),
    labels = c("0" = "Non-Diabetic", "1" = "Diabetic")
  ) +
  geom_histogram(bins = 20, position = "dodge") +
  theme_minimal() +
  labs(
    title = "Age Distribution"
  )
plot2
ggsave("Age_Distribution.tiff", plot2)

```


```{r}
# ================================================================
# PHASE II(d): VISUALIZATION - BMI DISTRIBUTION
# ================================================================

# Plot 3: Histogram of BMI Distribution by Diabetes Status
plot3 <- ggplot(Diabetes_inputed, aes(x = BMI, fill = factor(Outcome))) +
  scale_fill_manual(
    name = "Diabetes Status",
    values = c("0" = "blue4", "1" = "red4"),
    labels = c("0" = "Non-Diabetic", "1" = "Diabetic")
  ) +
  geom_histogram(bins = 20, position = "dodge") +
  theme_minimal() +
  labs(
    title = "BMI Distribution"
  )
plot3
ggsave("BMI_Distribution.tiff", plot3)

```



```{r}
# ================================================================
# PHASE II(e): VISUALIZATION - BOXPLOTS BY DIABETES STATUS
# ================================================================

# Plot 4a: Boxplot of Glucose by Diabetes Status
# Shows median, quartiles, and outliers for each group
plot4a <- ggplot(Diabetes_inputed, aes(x = factor(Outcome), y = Glucose, fill = factor(Outcome))) +
  scale_fill_manual(
    name = "Diabetes Status",
    values = c("0" = "blue4", "1" = "red4"),
    labels = c("0" = "Non-Diabetic", "1" = "Diabetic")
  ) +
  geom_boxplot() +
  theme_minimal() +
  labs(
    title = "Glucose by Diabetic Status",
    x = "Outcome"
  )

# Plot 4b: Boxplot of BMI by Diabetes Status
plot4b <- ggplot(Diabetes_inputed, aes(x = factor(Outcome), y = BMI, fill = factor(Outcome))) +
  scale_fill_manual(
    name = "Diabetes Status",
    values = c("0" = "blue4", "1" = "red4"),
    labels = c("0" = "Non-Diabetic", "1" = "Diabetic")
  ) +
  geom_boxplot() +
  theme_minimal() +
  labs(
    title = "BMI by Diabetic Status",
    x = "Outcome"
  )

# Plot 4c: Boxplot of Age by Diabetes Status
plot4c <- ggplot(Diabetes_inputed, aes(x = factor(Outcome), y = Age, fill = factor(Outcome))) +
  scale_fill_manual(
    name = "Diabetes Status",
    values = c("0" = "blue4", "1" = "red4"),
    labels = c("0" = "Non-Diabetic", "1" = "Diabetic")
  ) +
  geom_boxplot() +
  theme_minimal() +
  labs(
    title = "Age by Diabetic Status",
    x = "Outcome"
  )

# Display the individual plots
plot4a
plot4b
plot4c

# Save individual plots
ggsave("Glucose by Diabetic Status.tiff", plot4a)
ggsave("BMI by Diabetic Status.tiff", plot4b)
ggsave("Age by Diabetic Status.tiff", plot4c)

```


```{r}
# ================================================================
# PHASE II(f): WRAP ALL PLOTS TOGETHER
# ================================================================

# Load patchwork for combining multiple ggplot objects into a single figure
library(patchwork)

# Combine all plots into one composite figure with a title
all_plot <- plot1 + plot2 + plot3 + plot4a + plot4b + plot4c +
  plot_annotation(title = "Variable Distribution")

# Save the composite figure as a high-resolution TIFF
# width and height in inches, dpi = 300 for publication quality
ggsave("All plots.tiff", all_plot, width = 12, height = 8, dpi = 300)
```


```{r}
# ================================================================
# PHASE II(g): CORRELATION ANALYSIS
# ================================================================

# Load corrplot for correlation matrix visualization
library(corrplot)

# Plot 5: Correlation Analysis
# Select only numeric predictor variables (exclude the outcome column)
numeric_data <- Diabetes_inputed %>%
  select(-Outcome)  # -Outcome selects all columns except Outcome

# Compute correlation matrix for all numeric predictors
cor_matrix <- cor(numeric_data)

# Display correlation matrix rounded to 2 decimal places
round(cor_matrix, 2)

# Save correlation plot as TIFF for publication
tiff("Correlation.tiff", 
     res = 300,
     width = 9, 
     height = 9,
     units = "in",
     compression = "lzw")

# Create correlation plot with colored cells and upper triangular layout
corrplot(cor_matrix, method = "color", type = "upper")
dev.off()  # Close the graphics device to save the file

```


```{r}
# ================================================================
# PHASE II(h): SUMMARY TABLE BY OUTCOME
# ================================================================

# Compute mean values of key variables grouped by diabetes status
# This provides descriptive statistics for the main clinical characteristics
mean_f_4 <- Diabetes_inputed %>%
  group_by(Outcome) %>%
  summarise(
    Mean_Age = mean(Age),
    Mean_Glucose = mean(Glucose),
    Mean_BMI = mean(BMI),
    Mean_Insulin = mean(Insulin)
  )

# Reshape data from wide to long format for easier plotting
# This transformation prepares the data for grouped bar charts
data_frame_mean <- data.frame(mean_f_4) %>%
  pivot_longer(cols = -Outcome, names_to = "Variables", values_to = "Mean")
data_frame_mean

```


```{r}
# ================================================================
# PHASE II(i): VISUALIZATION - MEAN CHARACTERISTICS COMPARISON
# ================================================================

# Plot 6: Grouped bar chart comparing mean clinical characteristics
# This visualizes the summary table from above
plot5 <- ggplot(data_frame_mean, aes(x = Variables, y = Mean, fill = factor(Outcome))) +
  geom_col(position = "dodge") +
  theme_minimal() +
  coord_flip() +  # Flip coordinates for easier reading of variable names
  scale_fill_manual(
    name = "Diabetes Status",
    values = c("0" = "blue4", "1" = "red4"),
    labels = c("0" = "Non-Diabetic", "1" = "Diabetic")
  ) +
  labs(
    title = "Mean characteristics Comparison",
    x = "Outcome"
  )
plot5
ggsave("Mean characteristics Comparison.tiff", plot5)

```


```{r}
# ================================================================
# PHASE III(a): FORMAL STATISTICAL TESTING (T-TESTS)
# ================================================================

# Independent-samples t-tests to compare means of diabetic and non-diabetic patients
# Each test compares Group 0 (non-diabetic) vs Group 1 (diabetic)

# Test 1: Age
t.test(Age ~ Outcome, data = Diabetes_inputed)

# Test 2: Glucose
t.test(Glucose ~ Outcome, data = Diabetes_inputed)

# Test 3: BMI
t.test(BMI ~ Outcome, data = Diabetes_inputed)

# Test 4: Insulin
t.test(Insulin ~ Outcome, data = Diabetes_inputed)

# ================================================================

```


```{r}
# PHASE III(b): EFFECT SIZE ESTIMATION (COHEN'S D)
# ================================================================

# Load effsize package for Cohen's d calculation
library(effsize)

# Calculate Cohen's d for each variable
# Cohen's d measures the standardized difference between two means
# Values: 0.2 = small, 0.5 = medium, 0.8 = large, >1.0 = very large
cohen.d(Age ~ Outcome, data = Diabetes_inputed)
cohen.d(Glucose ~ Outcome, data = Diabetes_inputed)
cohen.d(BMI ~ Outcome, data = Diabetes_inputed)
cohen.d(Insulin ~ Outcome, data = Diabetes_inputed)

```


```{r}
# ================================================================
# PHASE III(c): VISUALIZATION - COHEN'S D EFFECT SIZES
# ================================================================

# Create a data frame with Cohen's d values for each predictor
# These values are manually entered from the output above
cohens_d <- data.frame(
  Predictors = c("Age", "Glucose", "BMI", "Insulin"),
  effsize = c(0.51, 1.19, 0.69, 0.44)
)
cohens_d

# Plot 6: Bar chart of Cohen's d effect sizes
# This visualizes which variables have the largest practical significance
plot6 <- ggplot(cohens_d, aes(x = Predictors, y = effsize, fill = Predictors)) +
  geom_col() +
  theme_minimal() +
  labs(title = "Cohen's d Effect Sizes", x = "Predictors", y = "Cohen's d")
plot6
ggsave("Cohen d.tiff", plot6)

```


```{r}
# ================================================================
# PHASE IV(a): LOGISTIC REGRESSION MODELING
# ================================================================

# Logistic Regression: Determines which variables remain significant predictors
# of diabetes after adjusting for all other variables

# Load sjPlot for model visualization (coefficient plots, tables)
# This package provides tab_model() and plot_model() for publication-ready outputs
library(sjPlot)

# Fit logistic regression model
# Outcome is binary (0 = non-diabetic, 1 = diabetic)
# Predictors include all clinical and demographic variables
logi_model <- glm(
  Outcome ~ Pregnancies + Glucose + BloodPressure + SkinThickness +
            Insulin + BMI + DiabetesPedigreeFunction + Age,
  Diabetes_inputed,
  family = binomial(link = "logit")
)

# Display model summary
# Shows coefficients, standard errors, z-values, p-values, and deviance statistics
summary(logi_model)

# Display odds ratios (exponentiated coefficients)
# OR > 1 indicates increased risk; OR < 1 indicates decreased risk
exp(coef(logi_model))

# Display odds ratios with 95% confidence intervals
# Provides a range of plausible values for each odds ratio
exp(confint(logi_model))

# Coefficient plot with 95% confidence intervals for all predictors
# This visualizes which variables are statistically significant
# (Those whose confidence intervals do not cross zero)
tab_model(logi_model)  # Gives OR, CI, p-values in a formatted table

# Create coefficient plot for publication
confi_plot <- plot_model(logi_model, title = "Coefficient plot with 95% confidence intervals for all predictors") +
  theme_sjplot2()
ggsave("Confidence interval.tiff", confi_plot, width = 12, height = 8, dpi = 300)

```


```{r}
# ================================================================
# PHASE IV(b): PREDICTED PROBABILITIES AND CLASSIFICATION
# ================================================================

# Generate predicted probability of diabetes for every patient
pred_prob <- predict(logi_model, type = "response")

# Display first few predicted probabilities
head(pred_prob)

# Convert probabilities into class predictions using threshold 0.5
# If probability > 0.5, classify as diabetic (1); otherwise non-diabetic (0)
pred_class <- ifelse(pred_prob > 0.5, 1, 0)

# Display first few predicted classes
head(pred_class)

```


```{r}
# ================================================================
# PHASE IV(c): CONFUSION MATRIX AND PERFORMANCE METRICS
# ================================================================

# Confusion Matrix: Compare actual outcomes with predicted outcomes
# Shows correct and incorrect classifications
confusion_matrix <- table(Actual = Diabetes_inputed$Outcome, Predicted = pred_class)
confusion_matrix

# Accuracy: Proportion of correctly classified patients
accuracy <- mean(pred_class == Diabetes_inputed$Outcome)
accuracy

# Sensitivity (True Positive Rate): Proportion of actual diabetics correctly identified
# TP / (TP + FN) — higher sensitivity means fewer missed diabetics
sensitivity <- confusion_matrix[2, 2] / (confusion_matrix[2, 1] + confusion_matrix[2, 2])
sensitivity

# Specificity (True Negative Rate): Proportion of actual non-diabetics correctly identified
# TN / (TN + FP) — higher specificity means fewer false alarms
specificity <- confusion_matrix[1, 1] / (confusion_matrix[1, 1] + confusion_matrix[1, 2])
specificity

# ================================================================
```


```{r}
# PHASE IV(d): MULTICOLLINEARITY DIAGNOSTICS
# ================================================================

# Load car package for Variance Inflation Factor (VIF) calculation
library(car)

# Calculate VIF for all predictors
# VIF > 5 indicates moderate multicollinearity; VIF > 10 indicates serious concern
# Values close to 1 indicate no multicollinearity
vif(logi_model)
```



```{r}
# ================================================================
# PHASE V: RECEIVER OPERATING CHARACTERISTIC (ROC) CURVE
# ================================================================

# Load pROC package for ROC analysis and AUC calculation
library(pROC)

# Create ROC curve object
# Compares predicted probabilities with actual outcomes
roc_obj <- roc(response = Diabetes_inputed$Outcome, predictor = pred_prob)

# Display ROC curve object
# Shows AUC value and thresholds
roc_obj

# Display AUC value (Area Under the ROC Curve)
# AUC interpretation:
# 0.50 = No discrimination, 0.60-0.70 = Poor, 0.70-0.80 = Fair
# 0.80-0.90 = Good, 0.90-1.00 = Excellent
auc(roc_obj)

# Plot ROC curve as high-resolution TIFF for publication
tiff("ROC Curve for Diabetes Prediction Model.tiff", 
     res = 300,
     width = 5, 
     height = 5,
     units = "in",
     compression = "lzw")

# Create ROC plot with red curve
# The curve should be substantially above the diagonal (no-discrimination line)
plot(roc_obj, main = "ROC Curve for Diabetes Prediction Model", col = "red4")
dev.off()  # Close graphics device to save file
```
